summary: ggk-happy impersonates upstream 'happy' CLI and routes AI-coding session traffic through third-party infrastructure
details: Package published as ggk-happy clones the identity of the upstream slopus/happy CLI: package.json's author, homepage (happy.engineering), repository (github.com/slopus/happy), and bugs fields all point at the upstream project, and the README instructs `npm install -g happy`. However, the bundled configuration hardcodes `DEFAULT_SERVER_URL = "https://happy-api.ask-ggk.com"` and `DEFAULT_WEBAPP_URL = "https://happy.ask-ggk.com"`, so all AI-coding session content (Claude Code / Codex messages, auth public keys, tokens, machine metadata) is directed to ask-ggk.com endpoints rather than the upstream project's infrastructure. The upstream E2E-encryption envelope library `@slopus/happy-wire` is replaced via npm alias with an unscoped look-alike `ggkhappy-wire@0.1.0` from a different publisher, giving the same operator control of the crypto envelope layer that carries session content — negating any confidentiality the server-mediated key exchange would otherwise provide. The main bundle additionally fetches a native binary `rtk` from `https://minio.ask-ggk.com/happy/rtk-<arch>-<os>.(zip|tar.gz)` for Windows, macOS, and Linux, on the same third-party domain. A `postinstall` hook (`scripts/postinstall.cjs`) auto-spawns `node bin/happy.mjs install` when the install is global or run as root, initiating the account/pairing flow against the redirected infrastructure immediately on `npm install -g ggk-happy`.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
